md: The expression under the square root in the quadratic formula has a name and a job all its own. It's called the **discriminant**:
It tells you, *without solving anything*, how many real roots a quadratic $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$ has — and equivalently, how many times the parabola $y = ax^2 + bx + c$ crosses the x-axis.

blocks.title: The three cases
blocks.body:
**$\Delta > 0$** → two distinct real roots. The parabola crosses the x-axis at two different points.
**$\Delta = 0$** → one repeated real root. The parabola just *touches* the x-axis at its vertex.
**$\Delta < 0$** → no real roots. The parabola is entirely above (if $a > 0$) or below (if $a < 0$) the x-axis.

md: Try the slider widget — change $a$, $b$, $c$ and watch the discriminant change sign. See how the red roots appear, merge, then disappear:

Look at the quadratic formula: $x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$. The roots split into two values *because of the $\pm$*. But that only happens when $\sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}$ is a real, non-zero number.
If $b^2 - 4ac > 0$, the square root is positive, and $\pm$ gives two different answers.
If $b^2 - 4ac = 0$, the square root is zero — both signs give the same answer.
If $b^2 - 4ac < 0$, the square root of a negative number isn't real — no real solutions.
**$x^2 - 4x + 4 = 0$.** $\Delta = 16 - 16 = 0$ → one repeated root (the parabola touches the x-axis at its vertex).
**$x^2 + 1 = 0$.** $\Delta = 0 - 4 = -4 < 0$ → no real roots (parabola sits entirely above the x-axis).

blocks.title: Use it to save time
blocks.body: Before plowing through the quadratic formula, check the discriminant. If $\Delta < 0$ you can stop right there — the answer is 'no real solutions'. No need to compute $\dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{\text{negative}}}{2a}$.

md: When $\Delta$ is a *perfect square* (like $1, 4, 9, 16, 25, \ldots$), the roots come out **rational** — $\sqrt{\Delta}$ is a whole number, so the formula gives clean fractions. That's a hint the trinomial probably **factors over the integers**, and you could have used factoring instead. (Example above: $\Delta = 49 = 7^2$ — and indeed $2x^2 + 3x - 5 = (2x + 5)(x - 1)$.)
If $\Delta$ is positive but not a perfect square, the roots are *irrational* — factoring won't give integer-coefficient factors. Use the quadratic formula.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: $\Delta = b^2 - 4ac$. Three cases: $\Delta > 0$ → 2 real roots; $\Delta = 0$ → 1 repeated; $\Delta < 0$ → no real roots. Compute it first and save yourself work — and look for perfect squares to know when factoring will succeed.
